scanner_description: Detects prompt injection, PII leaks to LLM vendors, unsanitized AI output rendered as HTML, unsafe function-calling tools, cost/DoS vectors, and other AI-using-application risks.

rules.name: User Input Interpolated Into OpenAI Prompt (Template Literal)
rules.note: User-controlled input is interpolated directly into a message sent to OpenAI. Attackers can override your system instructions via prompt injection (e.g., 'Ignore previous instructions and...').
rules.remediation: Put user content in its own user-role message — never concatenate it into the system prompt or another user message. Add length limits and consider a refusal layer on the output. Reference: https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/

rules.name: User Input Interpolated Into Anthropic Prompt (Template Literal)
rules.note: User-controlled input is interpolated directly into a Claude message. Prompt-injection attackers can override your system prompt with crafted input.
rules.remediation: Pass user content via a separate user-role message. Use Anthropic's `system` parameter for instructions — never mix instructions and untrusted text in one string. Reference: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-guardrails

rules.name: User Input in System-Role LLM Message
rules.note: A system-role message contains an interpolated value from the request. User input belongs in the user role — putting it in the system role lets attackers rewrite the assistant's instructions.
rules.remediation: Keep the system message static. Pass all user content via { role: 'user', content: ... } so the model treats it as data, not instructions.

rules.name: Python f-string LLM Prompt With User Input
rules.note: An f-string concatenates user input directly into an LLM prompt. The same prompt-injection risk as JS template literals — user text can override system instructions.
rules.remediation: Pass user content as a separate `{'role': 'user', 'content': user_input}` message instead of f-stringing it into a wrapper. Validate length and consider a refusal layer on the output.

rules.name: System Prompt Hardcoded in Frontend Code
rules.note: A long system prompt sits in client-side bundle code. Anyone who opens DevTools can read it — that lets attackers craft targeted jailbreaks for your specific instructions.
rules.remediation: Move all system prompts to server-side code. The browser should only post raw user messages — the server attaches the system prompt before forwarding to the model.

rules.name: Email Address Pattern Sent to LLM Vendor
rules.note: Code constructs an LLM message that includes a raw email address pattern. Sending PII to OpenAI/Anthropic without redaction may violate GDPR, CCPA, or your own privacy policy.
rules.remediation: Redact or hash emails before sending to the model. If the LLM legitimately needs the email, document this in your privacy policy and enable your vendor's zero-retention setting.

rules.name: SSN Pattern in LLM Message Construction
rules.note: A US Social Security Number pattern appears in code that builds an LLM message. Sending SSNs to a third-party LLM vendor is almost certainly a compliance violation.
rules.remediation: Redact SSNs before any LLM call. If your use case truly requires SSN, you need a self-hosted model and a documented legal basis — not a public API.

rules.name: Credit Card Number Pattern in LLM Message Construction
rules.note: A 16-digit number consistent with a credit card pattern appears in an LLM call site. Sending card data to a third-party LLM vendor breaks PCI DSS — this alone is enough to invalidate compliance.
rules.remediation: Never send raw card numbers to an LLM. Use a tokenized reference (e.g., Stripe customer ID) instead. Mask all but the last four digits if you must show the user.

rules.name: LLM Response Rendered via innerHTML (XSS)
rules.note: Model output is written to innerHTML. The model can produce arbitrary HTML — including <script> tags, event handlers, or javascript: URLs — leading to XSS. A prompt-injected response turns into client-side code execution.
rules.remediation: Render LLM output as plain text (`textContent`). If you need formatting, run the output through DOMPurify or a strict markdown renderer with HTML disabled.

rules.name: LLM Response in dangerouslySetInnerHTML
rules.note: Model output flows into React's dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Any HTML the model produces is rendered unsanitized — including scripts and event handlers. Prompt injection escalates to stored XSS.
rules.remediation: Render LLM output as React children (text) or run it through DOMPurify before injecting. Never trust model output to be HTML-safe even if your system prompt forbids tags.

rules.name: Markdown LLM Output Rendered Without Sanitizer
rules.note: Model output is rendered as markdown without a sanitization step. Most markdown renderers allow inline HTML by default — the model can emit <script>, <iframe>, or onerror handlers, leading to XSS.
rules.remediation: Configure your markdown renderer to disable HTML (e.g., `marked.setOptions({ sanitize: true })`, `markdown-it({ html: false })`) AND pipe the output through DOMPurify before injecting into the DOM.

rules.name: LLM Function-Calling Tool Shells Out (child_process)
rules.note: A file that defines LLM function-calling tools also uses child_process / exec. If the tool handler invokes a shell with model-controlled arguments, the model can execute arbitrary commands on your server.
rules.remediation: Never give an LLM a 'run shell command' tool in production. If you need command-like behavior, expose narrow, parameterized tools (e.g., `list_files(directory)`) and validate the directory against an allowlist.

rules.name: LLM Function-Calling Tool Uses eval()
rules.note: A file that defines LLM tools also calls eval() or new Function(). Combined with function-calling, this is full RCE: the model produces a string, your code evaluates it.
rules.remediation: Remove eval/new Function entirely. If you need dynamic dispatch, use a switch statement keyed off the tool name with a hardcoded handler table.

rules.name: LLM Function-Calling Tool With Unrestricted Filesystem Write
rules.note: A file that defines LLM tools also uses fs.writeFile / fs.writeFileSync. If the model controls the path or content, it can overwrite source files, drop a webshell, or escape your data directory.
rules.remediation: Constrain writes to a single sandbox directory and validate the resolved path stays inside it. Use `path.resolve` and a `startsWith` check on the sandbox root. Never accept absolute paths from the model.

rules.name: Missing max_tokens on LLM Completion Call
rules.note: An OpenAI/Anthropic call without max_tokens lets the model emit responses up to the context window. One slow loop or recursive agent can burn through your monthly budget in minutes.
rules.remediation: Always set max_tokens (or max_output_tokens). Pick a value tied to your UX (e.g., 500 for a chat reply, 2000 for a summary). Add a per-user daily token budget at the application layer.

rules.name: Hardcoded Deprecated Model Snapshot
rules.note: A dated model snapshot (e.g., gpt-3.5-turbo-0613, gpt-4-0314, claude-2.0) is hardcoded. OpenAI and Anthropic deprecate snapshots on a published schedule — when yours sunsets, every call throws 404.
rules.remediation: Pin a current model alias (gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet-latest) instead of a dated snapshot, or read the model name from config so you can rotate without a code change. Subscribe to the vendor's deprecation feed.

rules.name: Model Name Read From User Input
rules.note: The model parameter is set from the request body/query. A malicious client can request the most expensive model on every call — a direct billing-DoS vector.
rules.remediation: Set the model server-side from a fixed allowlist. If users genuinely choose a model, map a short opaque ID (e.g., 'fast' / 'smart') to the real model name in your code.

rules.name: Streaming LLM Response Without Abort Signal
rules.note: A streaming completion is created without an AbortSignal. If the client disconnects, the request keeps streaming server-side — you pay for the full response and may exhaust your connection pool under load.
rules.remediation: Pass `signal: req.signal` (Express 5 / Fastify) or wire an AbortController to your route handler. Most SDKs accept a `signal` option on the create() call.

rules.name: LangChain Shell/Exec Tool Imported
rules.note: LangChain's ShellTool (or a Python equivalent) lets the agent execute arbitrary shell commands. In an internet-facing app this is functionally equivalent to RCE-as-a-service.
rules.remediation: Remove ShellTool from your agent's tool list. Build narrow, validated tools for the specific operations the agent actually needs (e.g., `list_files(dir)` with an allowlist, not a raw shell).

rules.name: LLM API Key in Client-Side Code
rules.note: OpenAI/Anthropic SDK is initialized with an API key inside a file that ships to the browser. The key will appear verbatim in your bundle — anyone with DevTools can extract it and run up your bill.
rules.remediation: Move all LLM calls to a server route. The frontend posts the user message to your backend; the backend adds the API key and forwards to the model. Rotate the leaked key after fixing.

rules.name: dangerouslyAllowBrowser Set on LLM SDK
rules.note: The OpenAI / Anthropic SDK exposes a `dangerouslyAllowBrowser: true` escape hatch. Setting it in client-side code intentionally bypasses the SDK's built-in API-key-leak protection — the key ends up in every user's browser bundle.
rules.remediation: Remove `dangerouslyAllowBrowser: true`. Run the SDK on a server route instead. If this is a developer-only tool, gate the bundle behind authentication and rotate the key on every release.

rules.name: Recursive Agent Loop Without Iteration Cap
rules.note: An agent loop calls the model repeatedly without a maximum-iteration guard. A self-prompting agent or a prompt-injection that says 'call yourself again' can spin until the budget runs out.
rules.remediation: Wrap the agent loop in `for (let i = 0; i < MAX_ITERATIONS; i++)` with a hard cap (typically 5–10). LangChain exposes `maxIterations` on AgentExecutor — set it explicitly.

rules.name: Embedding API Called Without Input Length Limit
rules.note: An embedding call passes user input directly to the model without length validation. A malicious 8000-token paste burns through your embedding budget and may exceed model context.
rules.remediation: Truncate or reject inputs over a fixed character/token budget before calling embeddings.create. Most embedding models cap at 8192 tokens — enforce a smaller app-level limit (e.g., 2000 tokens).
